title: How Marie Curie Discovered Radioactivity and Paid the Price

lesson.learn:
Marie Curie's work transformed science because she treated radioactivity as a measurable physical phenomenon, not a curiosity. Working with Pierre Curie, she studied uranium ores, isolated new elements, and helped prove that atoms can emit energy on their own. Her discoveries opened new paths in physics, chemistry, medicine, and cancer treatment.

But the price was enormous. Curie worked with radioactive material long before scientists understood the health risks, and the damage to her body eventually reflected the danger of the work itself.
lesson.simpler: Marie Curie showed that some elements naturally give off radiation. Her discoveries changed science, but the work was dangerous.
lesson.keyTakeaway: Curie made radioactivity visible to science, and her career showed both the power and the cost of discovery.
lesson.deeperDive:
Curie's breakthrough was not just finding radium and polonium. It was proving that radiation came from the atom itself. That changed how scientists thought about matter. She also organized laboratory work with remarkable precision, turning messy mineral samples into evidence. Her later life, including her wartime medical work, showed how discovery can serve humanity even when the danger is poorly understood.

The story of Curie is both triumphant and sobering. She expanded the frontiers of science, but she did so in an era before safety standards could protect her from the consequences.
lesson.funFact: Marie Curie carried radium samples in her pocket.

people: Pierre Curie - scientific partner and husband
Laboratory assistants - people who helped isolate elements
Medical researchers - later users of Curie's discoveries
places: Institut du Radium - center of her later work
Europe during World War I - where her medical devices were used
Radium laboratories - spaces where radiation was studied
years: 1903 - Curie wins the Nobel Prize in Physics
1911 - Curie wins the Nobel Prize in Chemistry
Present day - her work remains foundational
concepts: Element - a substance defined by atomic number
Ionizing radiation - high-energy radiation that can damage tissue
Isolation - separating a substance from a mixture
Scientific method - careful testing and measurement
